title: Share of people who have depressive disorders, by age
subtitle: Estimated share of people who have  [depressive disorders](#dod:depressive_disorders), whether or not they are diagnosed, based on representative surveys, medical data and statistical modelling.
caption:
Share of people who have depressive disorders, by age, World, 2023
Estimated share of people who have depressive disorders, whether or not they are diagnosed, based on representative surveys, medical data and statistical modelling.

This horizontal bar chart displays the estimated share of the global population with depressive disorders across specific age brackets for the year 2023. The data, derived from representative surveys, medical records, and statistical modeling, includes individuals regardless of whether they have received a formal diagnosis. The vertical axis lists age categories ranging from children aged 5-14 up to adults aged 70 and older, while the horizontal bars represent the percentage of people in each group affected by these disorders. The distribution shows a distinct pattern where prevalence remains low in childhood, rises significantly during adolescence, and stabilizes at a higher level throughout adulthood and old age. The lowest value appears in the 5-14 age group, where the share sits at 0.98%. A substantial increase occurs in the 15-19 bracket, which reaches 4.55%, followed by a continued rise through the 20-24 age group at 4.78% and the 25-29 group at 4.91%. The highest prevalence rates cluster among older adults, with the 65-69 age group recording the maximum value on the chart at 5.46%. The 60-64 and 70+ age groups follow closely with shares of 5.4% and 5.36% respectively. The age-standardized estimate for the total population stands at 3.81%, while the unadjusted figure for all ages is 4%. The data shows that the share of people with depressive disorders is lowest in early childhood and highest in the older adult population, with a sharp increase occurring between the ages of 14 and 19.
